source.title: A fast pathway for fear in human amygdala

confidence_rationale: Direct human intracranial recordings (11 epilepsy patients with depth electrodes in amygdala and visual cortex) providing millisecond-resolution latency data. Exceptionally rare methodology, addressing the long-standing critique that earlier support for a low road was only fMRI (which lacks ms resolution). Response selectivity to low spatial frequency fearful faces (predicted by magnocellular pathway) strengthens mechanistic interpretation. Main caveat: small N and epilepsy population. Does not rule out additional cortical routes, but does establish that a fast pre-cortical route exists.
failure_signatures: If CeAAnalog.tick() waits for cortical (AIC/dACC) inputs before emitting mode_prior, the fast-prime signal arrives too late to bias action selection during the first ~100 ms window where the data show amygdala is already committed.

If fast_prime is gated only by high spatial frequency / detailed perceptual features, the magnocellular/low-spatial-frequency selectivity observed here will not be reproduced — CeA in REE should be driven by a coarse feature stream, not the fully decoded z_world.
If CeA responds uniformly to arousal (scenes), the social/threat-selectivity in the data is violated — CeA feature gate must be selective, not generic saliency.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Human amygdala shows a selective electrophysiological response to fearful faces beginning ~74 ms post-stimulus, preceding fear-related discrimination in visual cortex (which emerges only at ~400 ms and only for high-spatial-frequency content). The fast response is limited to low spatial frequency components, consistent with a magnocellular subcortical route, and is absent for arousing non-social scenes.
mapping.ree_translation: CeAAnalog module produces mode_prior (fast salience signal to SalienceCoordinator, MECH-046) AND fast_prime (low-latency action bias, MECH-074c). The 74 ms vs 400 ms split calibrates CeA.tick() as a first-response salience stage that must fire before AIC/dACC complete their computation. Concretely: CeA should run on a coarse (low-spatial-frequency analogue) feature of z_harm_a and emit fast_prime within 1-2 sim steps of stimulus arrival, well before cortical analogues finish integrating. fast_prime should bias action selection via a pre-softmax log-odds adjustment on the SalienceCoordinator input, not post-softmax scaling, to mirror a true early commitment rather than a late reweighting. Activation threshold: CeA should fire when a coarse magnitude of the harm-affective latent (||z_harm_a_coarse|| or the L1-norm of the low-frequency band of z_harm_a) exceeds a setpoint tuned so that the CeA false-positive rate matches the ~25-30%% false alarms typical of a low-threshold first-pass filter. Fast-prime does NOT gate SalienceCoordinator entirely; it biases it, consistent with downstream cortical integration overriding CeA in non-threat contexts.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The 74 ms is from fearful-face stimuli — a socially tuned evolutionary category. REE's CeA analogue will not have face-specific magnocellular inputs; we map the principle (coarse/fast/affectively-valent feature → early CeA response) rather than the face-specificity. Also: human epilepsy patients may not generalize to healthy cortex, and cortical latency numbers here are for fear-specific discrimination, not for cortex activation per se (which starts earlier but is not fear-selective until ~400 ms).
mapping.source_context: 11 pharmaco-resistant epilepsy patients undergoing intracranial monitoring at Hospital Ruber Internacional (Madrid). Depth electrodes in basolateral amygdala and occipital/temporal cortex. Stimuli: fearful vs neutral vs happy faces, plus arousing scenes control. Analyses: event-related potentials + time-frequency on local field potentials. Published Nat Neurosci 2016, PMID 27294508.
